sentences.text: No.
I-- at this moment, I didn't have any inkling of it yet.
By now, it is-- although by now it is somewhere in the beginning of April, 1945.
About five weeks before the end of the war or something like that.
But I didn't-- I don't realize that.
The one thing is, now here I am walking around and was able to get some of my strength back-- still pretty skinny but nevertheless.
And I was kind of talking to a man, who was a Christian prisoner, who was laying in a bunk with a broken leg.
He must have been one of the more privileged guys.
He was in pretty good shape, very strong looking.
And I don't know really whether he worked in the kitchen or something like official.
But he was in good shape.
And I started to talk to him, to befriend him.
And we talked.
I'd sit by his bed very often.
He had a big stool standing there, one of those bar stools.
Like real, heavy, wooden stool.
Will Kusek was his name.
And I talked to him--
Will Kusek.
And I talked to him.
Suddenly, the speaker announces that all Jews are supposed to be assembled outside in marching formation.
And I said, oh my gosh, well, that's it.
Because we didn't own anything, you know?
You were ready to go at any-- the way you were.
And so I said, did you hear that?
Now I got to go out.
And he grabs my arm.
And he says, you're not-- you're not going to go out there.
I says, wait a minute, didn't you understand?
All Jews have to be-- he says, look.
Your marking is not there anymore.
Until recently, everybody was marked.
Jewish-- Jewish prisoners had a yellow triangle on their uniform, and mine was worn off already.
They didn't enforce it anymore.
Nobody knew anymore who was who, really.
And he says, look.
They don't know anything about you, so you stay here.
Because I can tell you are not-- that you don't want to go on that march.
He knew something that I didn't expect.
And so I said, no.
I can't do that.
Look, I'm Jewish, and I'm not going to hide the fact.
That's a-- He sat up, and he grabbed my arm again.
And he was very strong, with my little weight.
And he said, look, get off that stool.
And he grabbed my arm and that stool.
He says, if you make one more step, I grab the stool and hit you over the head.
You can't go anywhere, he says.
And he started to lift that stool already.
And so-- well, he convinced me that I would stay.
And it turns out that this was another one of my incidents of my chain of rescue, of survival.
A link, part of the link.
That if I would have gone out, it turned out these people were all sent to Buchenwald and were all executed and-- and cremated.
I don't know.
He heard it through the grapevine.
He had some kind of a-- some kind of a--
Oh, that came out later.
That came out later.
And he told me later that that was planned.
And yes, the-- the-- is shown that that happened.
Yes.
So now, here I am.
And then hang in there for a while longer.
Suddenly, now we have-- we have heard already-- that-- that the-- that we heard from-- from-- from the noises of war, from the shootings that we heard that the American troops are coming closer.
And we figured, my gosh, they should come in any moment now, you know, and just rescue us.
And yes, lo and behold, suddenly we see the-- our SS guards loading up trucks and starting to move out of the camp.
They all moved out.
Leaving.
They are moving out of the camp.
And we figured, my gosh, that's-- isn't that a wonderful thing.
Finally that happens.
And some of the prisoners took white-- white sheets and put it on the roof because there were Allied fliers yet bombing the areas.
And they put sheets over there, wrote on-- wrote on there that-- with the KZ-- that's the German word for concentration, KZ.
Konzentrationslager, a concentration camp.
So that they would see that this is-- in case do it.
We were waiting for the Americans to come in.
Well, this happened in the morning.
In the middle of the afternoon, while we were waiting for the Americans, the SS guards come in with the trucks.
And we heard the shooting is sort of subsiding, moving away from the America-- that we thought were from the American troops.
And all of a sudden, now SS guards were in.
Soon afterwards, the next morning, they ordered us out to form marching columns to be marched out of the camp.
Yes.
No, no.
The same, the same.
They all had left.
They all returned into the camp now.
Yeah.
They all returned, came back into the camp.
And naturally, you can imagine the consternation that we felt.
And so the-- the next morning, we were ordered, as I said, in marching columns.
To build columns of 1,000 men each.
It had to-- happened to be that I was in the first one.
And we were now-- because again, the same reason as in Auschwitz-- there were 140,000 prisoners left here.
And to make-- to-- they were all condemned to die, practically.
And so to make a big pile with machine guns of 140,000 prisoners, it's quite a-- quite a pile that they didn't want to leave to the world.
And they knew that they had to give up the camp.
And so therefore, they came up with the fiendish idea of the death marches, which had been employed almost in every other camp, too.
And so we were put into the column, and we were now moving out.
And we were marching now between the American lines, back and forth.
The American lines tried to encircle the German contingent.
And what like maybe three, four, five miles from one side to another we came there, the shooting got closer, we turned around, marched back.
And so-- after-- nothing to eat at all we received.
We actually pulled grass out of the ground-- all dried-up grass.
So that now it is April.
April 20th of 1945.
The new grass hadn't grown yet.
And so we are marching and marching.
And all of sudden, we see that-- no.
Let me put it this way.
Now, you've got to imagine the group of marchers there.
Most of them-- most of them were literally skeletons.
Skeletons with bones and skin pulled over who tried to drag themselves along according to the speed as the guards on our side provided.
And this was not like a Sunday afternoon stroll, I can assure you.
And of course, these weak, wretched souls would stumble over the slightest pebble on the road that we marched on and would fall to the ground.
At which moment, the guard came and would shoot him in the head.
And at the end of the column was a truck that picked up.
The bodies were picked up and thrown onto the truck.
And this was a death march.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes, I was right in it.
And-- and so it was constant.
You heard a shot next to your side constantly.
You couldn't-- you couldn't-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you didn't have the strength or the thought to-- to-- to help, because you had everything to do to keep yourself alive and upright.
And by the way, when we were liberated-- just to jump ahead for a moment-- I was weighed, and I weighed 82 pounds.
And with that I was not the skinniest guy on the block-- guy on the block by any means.
There were guys really that couldn't have weighed more than 60, 65 pounds.
You couldn't believe that these guys are still moving along, trying, knowing that-- that-- that liberation is near.
Trying to hold themselves up as much as possible.
Didn't have a chance.
Didn't have the strength, and they just succumbed.
And so therefore, I was trying to fight my hallucination.
I started to hallucinate.
And I knew when somebody hallucinated, they usually soon lost their control and would fall, and then the shot would come out.
And so I started to hallucinate.
And I tried to talk to myself, hey, don't let it happen.
Talk to myself.
And as it turned-- as it was-- it turned out that some guy behind me hollered, hey, guys, look.
Turn around.
And I turned around, and I saw what I still consider the most beautiful sight of my life-- a contingent of American armored vehicles coming down the hillside with the American insignia on it.
And our guards, of course, at that moment, took off in all directions.
And then we stopped.
Stopped short at a moment.
The commander of the American outfit stopped in front of us and directed us back to the-- to the one road which-- where there was an American Red Cross contingent.
He says, it's just a quarter of a mile up that one road.
There they are there.
We are waiting for you already.
Sorry we couldn't come any sooner to you, he said.
And so-- and so-- and he made a remark which sounded to us utterly-- utterly ridiculous.
He says, now, anybody that wants to walk that quarter mile, just go that way.
Not this or that.
And so we said, my gosh, walking.
And he said, otherwise there will be trucks and jeeps to pick you up, don't worry about it.
Anyways, but while these vehicles were coming by, they were shooting out of their turrets.
They were actually a fighting contingent, fighting the Germans.
They are shooting.
But while they were riding by and shooting, they opened the hatches, and threw out all kinds of ration boxes, the military ration boxes.
And of course, we all ran as much as we could and grabbed one.
And you could see some guys just-- as I, too, tried to open it right away.
And some guys opened it.
And regretfully-- some guys opened it, I meant to say, and ate everything inside.
And regretfully, with this humane reaction by these wonderful soldiers that drove by, many, many people died because of that.
Of course, they couldn't.
And they were fighting, too.
They couldn't-- look, there was no doctor in every armored vehicle that said, hey, listen, these guys are not good.
Don't give that to them.
They might eat it and might die.
Exactly what happened, regretfully.
And so I saw a guy next to me opened up.
And there was a can.
I think it was butter.
There was oil swimming on top.
And he just slurped the stuff down, the whole thing.
And meats, meat-- cans with meat.
These guys would slurp them.
Their bodies of 60 to 80 pounds, including my-- couldn't-- just couldn't take it.
So many, many of these people regretfully--
Pardon?
Fortunately, my brain worked always like-- many of the others, too, I'm not trying to tell you that I'm the only one.
Most of the others had the same thoughts.
I opened that box.
And I was looking for something benign, you know.
And I looked for it.
And I came upon a package which I thought were some kind of a cookies.
Looked wonderfully packed, nicely shiny wrapping.
And I opened this, and I ate it.
And it was-- at that time, I decided it was the most wonderful cookie I'd ever eaten in all my life.
I had never seen this kind of thing like that before nor eaten it, of course.
And so it looked dry.
And so I ate a few of those, and I started to-- to feel better right away.
And there was one thing I couldn't resist.
There was a-- there was chocolate in there, too.
And I broke a piece of chocolate off.
I knew a whole bar might be too dangerous.
So I ate that piece of chocolate.
I had dreamed about chocolate for the last four or five years, I guess.
Never had seen any.
But anyways, so this is what I did.
Now suddenly, there was not-- another-- now, this guy, by the way, that I talked about on the bunk that wanted to hit me with that stool over the head--
Yeah, Will Kusek.
I said it.
Will Kusek.
Yeah.
Yeah.
He and I walked together there now.
And we sat together.
And he was in better shape than I was, you know.
He's a Christian man, yeah.
And-- and so all of a sudden, we had eaten something.
And I said, you know something?
This man said we can't-- we don't have to walk.
All of a sudden, I said-- and now it starts to occur to me, after I had something my stomach, that I'm a free man.
And I said, wait a minute.
The guy said-- which I didn't mention, I presume-- if you want to walk that quarter mile, take your time.
As long as you want to.
We have Jeeps and trucks going up and down in case you change your mind.
You have to hop on, don't worry about it.
And so Will and I talked.
And he said, how do you feel about walking?
You know something?
We now can walk in the speed that we want to walk, you know?
If we make a few steps and we want to sit down, we sit down, don't we?
Can't we do that now?
We were just-- just like little children that learned to walk.
All of a sudden, it occurred to us that that is possible.
And so we decided to walk.
And we decided that quarter mile.
And it took us nearly four hours, I believe or more to walk that quarter mile.
In the meantime, the Jeeps came up and down that road.
I think we were the last ones in.
And they were probably were tired of--
Because we wanted to walk.
We enjoyed our walking.
We enjoyed the freedom.
You know, just-- just like a newborn animal that tries to make its new steps first of all.
It collapses for a moment, but it starts walking again.
And so that was the most wonderful thing because you see-- something I didn't mention, you don't realize.
First of all, you know that we were-- had to walk in the speed that we wanted to-- that the guards wanted do.
But in camp, when you were ordered to go from here to there, you had to run.
You never were allowed to walk.
And there were guards standing like every couple yards away from you on from both sides.
If you didn't run, you would be beaten by a stick.
And in fact, they were swinging it, and it was a game that you had to duck it and get out of the way.
You always had to run.
You never were allowed to walk, unless you were somewhere where the guards were not around, you know, in the barrack.
Inside the barrack or something.
Something in there, too.
So now we had this-- this-- this privilege of doing it the way we wanted to.
And that's what we did.
And finally, we made it.
And I approached-- I was stopped.
We were stopped.
Well, I was separate.
My friend went to another guard.
They were-- there was a tall Red Cross guy in front of me, American Red Cross uniformed man.
And he wanted to have my food box.
That's when-- that was the moment I lost it, you know?
No, this was a prized possession.
You will never get your hands on that, you know.
No way.
Why did he want it?
Well, he tried explain to me.
Now, his-- his German was practically nonexistent and so was my English.
And finally, eventually, after he told me that I can have all the other boxes, he put my name on it.
I-- that's what I made out he meant.
He took his pencil.
Started to write my name.
You can have all these, but give me that.
And then he explained to me that this food will be taken away from me because that's not good for me.
And so finally then, I-- I got my senses back.
Then I noticed what he wanted.
In fact, I tried to push this guy away from me. 82 guy and this guy was-- my 82 pounds guy and this guy was maybe 250 pounds-- a huge guy.
And so I was wondering why he didn't budge for a moment.
But then it all came in focus.
And that's when I actually collapsed, too.
And then I was on my back for about a week.
And they fed us, actually, with broth and light things.
Very, very much controlled.
And at the end then, after a week he said, here's your box back.
You want some more, he says?
Now how did you-- then how did you get reunited with your brother?
OK.
Now, my brother when we were separated, my brother assumed-- and absolutely correctly-- that in the condition that I was, I had no chance to make it.
He gave me maybe a day or two, then I would be trampled to death in the squeezing and stuff like that.
Absolutely correct.
I would have looked at it the identical way.
I was just condemned.
And he didn't even look for me for that reason.
He was 100% sure-- not knowing what happened to me in the meantime, of course-- that I hadn't made it.
Until some mutual acquaintance-- he stayed in Munich.
He didn't even want to go back to Berlin.
Until some mutual acquaintance pointed out to me that he had seen me, which he had a heck of a time to convince them that it was me.
Well, first of all, we couldn't see each other.
That's-- that's a funny thing.
I was in Berlin and he-- and he was in Munich.
And there was no telephone.
There was nothing that you could do.
No telephone, even letter writing didn't work very much.
This-- this-- this person that we talked to told me that my brother, being a cantor, was immediately doing his duty as a cantor, because there were no other cantors alive anymore.
And would sing.
And he would also do Friday night services over the radio.
And I did the same thing.
Well, not regularly over the radio, but I was doing one Friday night-- part of a Friday night service over the radio.
And somehow, he was able to turn the radio on in Munich and to hear me, and vice versa.
I heard-- yeah.
Yeah, and I heard him from Munich doing his thing.
So that's the first time how we heard each other.
How we were able to hearing each other, I mean, of course.
And so then soon after that, my wife and I snuck out over the-- over the Russian-controlled borders.
It was a very dangerous thing at that time.
The Russians were very, very dangerous.
If they would catch you out of your premises, too, they would nail you.
And so we went to Munich to visit my brother and his wife.
Well, how we happened to meet?
My wife and I, we knew each other from before, because her mother was friends with the parents of a friend of mine.
And so during these sessions that I told you, when we were always together with youth groups, even the adults would meet each other like as the Germans say, they call a coffee klatch.
They would meet there and the women would talk, and the men would play card games or something like that.
And they would sit.
Because you couldn't go anyplace, as I said.
Remember that.
And so we, the young-- our young group, the musicians group, we met and went into another room and had our fun there.
Now my wife, Inge, was-- and I'm talking at the time when I was 17 years old. 16, 17, 18-year-old during that time.
And since she's a few years older, she didn't look much like a-- a woman to me.
She looked to me at the time like a little girl who was the daughter of one of our mutual friends.
And so I didn't pay too much attention to her, I must admit, at the time I went through.
And so-- but then when I came back-- she fortunately was not in the concentration camp, although she was put in slavery work conditions because she had a Jewish mother.
Her father was Christian.
And therefore, she had mildly-- mildly said, 'tainted blood.' And was not allowed to work in any position that she wanted to.
She did work as a secretary and was not allowed to work any further.
She was then on-- put on construction work.
In those days, you didn't see many women on construction work.
So--
Yeah.
And so when after the war when I came back, I saw her mother-- who was also in the concentration camp.
Sent to the concentration camp.
In the first one, Terezin.
And as it was, if you saw any Jewish person on the street anywhere, you would ask, have you heard about so-and so?
Have you heard about so-and-so?
Have you heard about so-and-so?
And her mother came out of a movie theater with-- with her other daughter, my wife's sister.
And I was always one of those guys.
I didn't want to be late when a movie starts.
I wanted to be in there at the beginning.
And it was just about to start.
I was there.
I was on the way into the movie with a friend of mine.
And we wanted to get in there very quick.
And I said, you know, Mrs.
Fromm-- at the time, Fromm was her name-- have you heard about so-and-so?
And you know something-- and she said, you know something, there's not much time now.
Why don't you come and visit-- visit me?
I knew where she was living.
And that's what I did.
I visited her.
And then I saw her other daughter, namely my-- what's now my wife-- again.
And she had changed considerably.
[LAUGHTER] And so-- so that's why-- how I got the idea maybe we can get together.
Yeah.
Yes, fortunately.
Thank goodness, yes.
My brother and his wife were married in the concentration camp of Terezin.
As odd as it sounds, it was possible there.
Yes, under certain circumstances.
And fortunately, she made it.
They were also separated when we were sent to Auschwitz.
She was also, I think, sent to Auschwitz but then to another camp.
Yeah, she was liberated from another camp.
Well, first of all, of course, as you correctly assumed, we got married in 1946.
In September 1946.
And immediately, I mentioned right away, and she was also in agreement.
And her wish was, too, to leave Germany as soon as possible.
So eventually, we arrived in America.
Regretfully, later than we wanted to, because I lived in the French sector of Berlin.
And the French at that time didn't allow anybody to leave the area no matter where you wanted to go.
If we would have lived in the English or British-- English or American sector, yes.
We could have moved-- gotten out practically immediately.
But it was also not easy to move into another sector.
They wanted to have head exchange.
In other words, the Allies fed the people in those sectors, and they don't want to have additional mouths to feed.
Or in this case, heads to feed as it may be.
Because it was called [GERMAN], head exchange.
And so we were-- we couldn't move into the other sector.
Nobody wanted to get out of the American and English sector.
But finally, then we managed by buying off-- now I'm telling you that I was a criminal.
By buying off a Berlin official who would fake this head exchange for a few hundred dollars-- for a few hundred marks.
And we did that although we didn't have much.
We just had barely saved that much together.
Because I came out of the concentration camp with my striped clothes, practically.
So that was it.
Nothing, no money, nothing.
So we-- we-- did the-- we paid this man off.
And that way we were allowed with falsified papers to move into the English sector of Berlin.
But of course, we were there in that English sector finally maybe four or five weeks.
Then the French, by that time, allowed everybody to move out the way they wanted to do.
But anyways, we have no bad feelings about that.
So therefore, we arrive now in America, in New York with the ship.
I got nicely seasick.
Worse, apparently, than my wife.
Although she was seasick, too.
And we had our little daughter there with us, who was one year old.
I don't think she noticed anything of that ride.
So we arrived in New York.
Now, New York, as we knew, is a melting pot.
We were earmarked to go to St.
Paul.
Now, the reason for that was we came-- we were supported by an organization, the-- the-- the-- [INAUDIBLE], I believe.
No.
It was-- no.
The American Joint Contribution.
Joint-- I can't think of it right now.
Joint-- well, anyways, maybe I can later.
Now, they would pay everything.
They would pay the fare.
They would guarantee for a job.
And would also guarantee for five years for our being five years in America until we became citizens.
They would be responsible-- as it was at that time controlled by the state, by the USA-- they would be responsible for our welfare for all that time, for the five years.
We would not be a burden to the welfare if need be.
And so they also, therefore, said they reserve the right to send us where they want us to go, and that was St.
Paul.
Now, at that time-- big city slickers, Berliners, you know-- we couldn't see ourselves to live in a little hick town like that, what we thought.
And so we tried to stay in New York.
When we arrived there, we had a friend there that was arriving there about a couple years before we were.
And when we went to the authorities to try to let us stay there, because we felt that we can manage there.
We didn't speak English at that time either.
And so the guy-- the official said, you know something?
You can stay here.
But if you have any problems, you've got to your friend, because this is not transferable.
Your budget is in St.
Paul.
And then he said the wise word.
He said, look.
Go to St.
Paul.
Once you are settled, you can go anyplace you want to if you don't like it there.
Well, this was what-- how long ago?
In 1949, which is now 46 years ago, and we are still in St.
Paul.
[LAUGHS]
And we are glad about it.
I started immediately.
Immediately to work as a-- as a furniture maker here in St.
Paul.
That was my-- my work.
I worked there for three years.
And then this-- this firm had to close down.
This was the only place around here where this kind of work that I knew.
Blueprint work and starting from scratch.
A furniture piece all made by hand.
Tremendously expensive.
They didn't get any customers anymore for that, you know, to pay thousands of dollars for a little piece of furniture.
Who wanted that?
And so I then started to sell furniture for a furniture store, one of the largest furniture store in St.
Paul.
Cardozo's was their name.
They're not any longer in existence, which is not my fault.
No.
But-- and so and then somebody that I sold furniture to was a member of the insurance business, managing-- a manager of Prudential.
And he put the bug into my ear to start selling insurance, which eventually I did.
And now I'm retired from Prudential Insurance.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Stephanie.
Yeah.
Oh, David came six and a half years later.
And his name is David.
He lives in Bloomington.
Our daughter lives here only half-- half a block away from us.
Well, it's probably the message that I'm trying to deliver to anybody that I am speaking to.
I'm speaking an awful lot in all kinds of universities here in the state and all out of the state.
An awful lot to high schools in the city, out of the city.
And I am always trying to let the message go through that it is time for people to recognize that the word is made out of many, many different people.
Different colors, different sizes, and all kinds of differences.
That it is about time that we recognize that.
It's about time that we learn to live with it.
And one way to learn to live with it is that-- and it is about time for that-- that we learn-- that we start to learn about each other.
And while we may not agree with the ideology and the lifestyle of other people, it is time by knowing about them to respect them as I expect them to respect me.
And if this can come down-- and I think we're making some progress.
Not a lot, but I see some progress here and there.
If this can come about, then I think the world will be a much better place.
And the letters and reactions that I get to my speaking shows an awful lot.
I get so many letters that people tell me, you know, I never looked at it that way.
And it's just a wonderful thing.
And from now on, I will be watching out for it.
And I-- there were many, many wonderful results that I have out of this.
And this is why I'm doing it.
And that's basically my main message.
Because I tell them, I am the prime example of what can happen to people that-- that are-- that are suffering under prejudicial circumstances and biases and when nobody speaks up.
We have to learn to speak up when we see prejudice and hatred.
This when we--
No, not--
Arrived in New-- oops, I'm sorry.
Oh, sorry.
Not first.
Fifth--
No.
Ten.
Oh.
Can you roll back a little?
This is on the troop transporter.
Taken on the troop transporter General Ballou when we arrived in New York.
This is my wife, Inge.
And our daughter, Stephanie, who was one year old then.
And of course, me.
This is the only picture that survived the past of my personal and my family's picture.
This is me on your right, in 1941.
I'm 20 years old on a bicycle tour.
This is my wife's uncle, Friedl is his name-- in the German uniform of World War I.
He was later, during the Nazi time, sent to Sachsenhausen concentration camp and died there.
This is the only picture that's in existence of my mom.
It happened to be in somebody's possession-- a friend of ours or something.
It happened to be also that I remember very, very well that this was the picture she always wanted to have destroyed.
And now this is the only one that is-- that's around.
Well, so--
